blocks.text: Before we write real programs, you need two tools: a way to save information, and a way to see it. That's it. Everything in JavaScript is built on top of those two things.

blocks.desc: Create a box called 'count' and put the number 1 inside it. The word 'let' means you're making a new box for the first time.
Look inside the box called 'count' and print whatever is in there. This is how you prove your code is working.

blocks.text: Here's a simple example of how to use variables and console.log:

lines: // 1. End every line with a semicolon  ;
// 2. Use 'let' the FIRST time you create a variable — never again after that

blocks.label: Cove says:
blocks.text: console.log is your best friend. Professional developers use it every single day. When something breaks, the first thing you do is console.log everything to figure out what's wrong.

challenges.title: Build your player card
challenges.description: Create three variables for a player card: their name, their starting score, and their favorite colour. Log all three. Then change the score to 99 and log it again.
challenges.hint: Log each variable with console.log. To change score, write score = 99 — no 'let' this time.
challenges.explanation: Variables are declared once with 'let'. To update a value later, just write the name without 'let' — otherwise JavaScript thinks you're making a new variable. console.log prints whatever is inside the variable at that moment, so logging before and after the change shows both values.

challenges.title: The scoreboard
challenges.description: A scoreboard needs to track a player's score as it changes. Start at 0, log it. Update to 50, log it. Update to 120, log it. Each log should show the current value at that moment.
challenges.hint: Use console.log(score) right after each change. To update score, write score = 50 — no 'let'.
challenges.explanation: You only use 'let' once — when the variable is first created. Every update after that is just score = newValue. console.log reads whatever is in the variable at that exact line, so the order matters: log before you change and you get the old value, log after and you get the new one.

exercises.title: What does 'let' do?
tasks: What does the word 'let' do in JavaScript?
exercises.hint: Think about what you're telling JavaScript to create.

options.label: Prints a value to the console

exercises.explanation: 'let' creates a new named container. Without it, there's nowhere to store the game's score, level, or playerName.

exercises.title: What does this print?
tasks: Read the code. What appears in the console?
exercises.hint: Look at what value is stored inside count before the log runs.
exercises.explanation: console.log looks inside the variable and prints what it finds. count holds 5, so 5 is printed. Writing 'count' in quotes would print the word count, not the number.

exercises.title: Correct or broken?
tasks: Drag each line into the correct bucket.
exercises.hint: Every variable needs: let, a name, an equals sign, and a value — in that order. JavaScript is also case-sensitive.
exercises.explanation: JavaScript is unforgiving about spelling and order. 'let' must be lowercase with no extra letters. console.log must be all lowercase. The value must exist after the equals sign.

exercises.title: Complete the variable
tasks: Drop the right tokens to declare playerName and set it to 'Alex'.
exercises.hint: Text values need quotes around them. The keyword that creates a variable is three letters.
exercises.explanation: 'let' creates the variable. The text value 'Alex' needs quotes — without them, JavaScript would look for a variable named Alex and find nothing.

exercises.title: Complete the console.log
tasks: Drop the right tokens to print the value inside score.
exercises.hint: The variable name goes inside the parentheses without quotes. Quotes would print the word, not the value.
exercises.explanation: console.log is the command. score without quotes tells JavaScript to look inside the container and print what it finds. 'score' in quotes would just print the text 'score'.

exercises.title: Reassign without 'let'
tasks: score already exists on line 1. Drop the right token to change it to 99 on line 2.
exercises.hint: You only use 'let' the very first time. After that, just write the name.
exercises.explanation: Reassignment uses just the variable name — no 'let'. Using 'let' again would tell JavaScript to create a new variable with the same name, which causes an error.

exercises.title: Type the keyword and value
tasks: Type the missing keyword and value to declare level starting at 1.
exercises.hint: The keyword that creates a new variable is three letters. The value is a number — no quotes needed.
exercises.explanation: 'let' creates the variable. 1 is the value stored inside it. Together they give Simon Says a container to track which level the player is on.

exercises.title: Put the code in order
tasks: Drag these lines into the correct order so the code runs without errors.
exercises.hint: You must declare a variable before you can use it. Logging after a change shows the new value.
exercises.explanation: The variable must exist before you log it. Logging before the change prints 1; logging after prints 2. That's why order matters — JavaScript runs top to bottom.

exercises.title: What prints second?
tasks: There are two console.logs. What does the second one print?
exercises.hint: Follow the code top to bottom. What is count equal to by the time the second log runs?
exercises.explanation: count starts as 3 (first log prints 3), then gets replaced by 8. The second log runs after the change, so it prints 8. Adding both values to get 11 is a common mistake — reassignment replaces, it does not add.

exercises.title: Your Simon Says variables
tasks: Declare two variables: playerName (set to your name in quotes) and level (set to 1).
Add a console.log for each one. Hit Run.
You should see both values printed in the console.
exercises.hint: Strings need quotes: let playerName = 'Alex'; Numbers don't: let level = 1;

steps.text: Declare let playerName = 'your name'; and let level = 1; Hit Run.
The game now has containers for the player's name and their current level.
Now add console.log(playerName); and console.log(level); below the variables. Hit Run.
You built the starting memory for a Simon Says game!

exercises.title: Watch a variable change
tasks: Hit Run. You'll see count printed twice — both times it says 1.
Write count = 10; on the empty line. Hit Run.
Now you'll see 1 first, then 10. The variable changed mid-program!
exercises.hint: Write count = 10; — no 'let' this time. count already exists.

steps.text: Hit Run. Read the console — count prints the same value twice because nothing has changed yet.
Write count = 10; on the empty line between the two logs. Hit Run.
You see 1 first, then 10. The variable changed and console.log caught it both times!
